steps.title: Understand what the hypothesis predicts
steps.content: Yanagisuru's team found that as vortex size increases, pressure fluctuations rise for narrow-headed fish but stay low for wide-headed fish. The second team's hypothesis says greater pressure fluctuations lead to better obstacle avoidance. Combining these: narrow-headed fish (high fluctuations) should avoid obstacles more often; wide-headed fish (low fluctuations) should bump into them more often. Supporting evidence must show that difference.

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
Test each choice against the predicted pattern — wide-headed fish bumping more than narrow-headed fish:
  • Choice A — 'some narrow-headed flat needlefish bumped into obstructions more often than other needlefish did': compares narrow-headed fish only to each other — says nothing about wide-headed fish and provides no cross-type comparison. ✗
  • Choice B — 'narrow-headed flat needlefish bumped into obstructions just as often as the wide-headed devil catfish did': equal collision rates contradict the hypothesis that greater fluctuations produce better avoidance. ✗
  • Choice C — 'wide-headed devil catfish bumped into obstructions more often than narrow-headed flat needlefish did': directly shows wide-headed (low fluctuations) > narrow-headed (high fluctuations) collisions, which is exactly what the hypothesis predicts. ✓
  • Choice D — 'the wide-headed devil catfish bumped into more than half of the obstructions': reports only the wide-headed fish's rate with no narrow-headed fish comparison, so the hypothesis cannot be confirmed. ✗
That matches choice C — C is correct.
